text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr A. Rahman
Southgate Health Centre

27 High Street, Southgate, London N14 6LD
Dear Dr A. Rahman
Jordan Ellis, DOB: 14-03-1990, Hospital No: H937524 NHS No. 7842195603
Flat 12, 3 Parkview Court, London N11 2QP 

Diagnoses/summary: Recurrent unclassified seizures with recent clinical deterioration; sleep disruption and night-shift work reported temporal association with worsening. Prior EEGs abnormal; MRI brain previously non-lesional. No definite structural aetiology identified.

Recent investigations: Prolonged video-EEG last week demonstrated Electrographic seizures frequent on EEG (~10/h), with mixed semiology reported by patient and witnesses. Interictal discharges were widespread without a consistent lateralising pattern. Routine bloods unremarkable (U&E, LFT, FBC within reference ranges). MRI brain (2023) reported no focal cortical dysplasia or hippocampal sclerosis.

Medication history: Previously tried and stopped due to lack of efficacy or tolerability—carbamazepine (mood change), levetiracetam (irritability), valproate (weight gain, tremor), lacosamide (dizziness), lamotrigine (partial response then plateau), gabapentin (sedation), clobazam intermittent (daytime somnolence). Not tried: topiramate, zonisamide, perampanel, brivaracetam, cenobamate, pregabalin, rufinamide, tiagabine, acetazolamide, primidone, retigabine.

Comorbidities: Migraine without aura (infrequent), mild asthma; no known cardiac disease. No family history of epilepsy reported. No prior status epilepticus.

Today’s review: I reviewed Jordan Ellis in clinic today, attending alone. Over the last 3–4 months there has been a clear deterioration in day-to-day seizure burden and postictal fatigue. Jordan Ellis reports starting regular night shifts on a rotating pattern (four nights on, three off) with curtailed sleep on transition days. They and their partner note more frequent brief events overnight and on early mornings after shift change, with increased confusion and tongue-biting on two occasions. Witness accounts describe abrupt behavioural arrest with loss of awareness lasting 30–60 seconds, sometimes clustering. There has been no major injury but several near-falls at home. Work colleagues have noticed lapses during tasks when sleep has been restricted. The recent EEG corroborates the clinical impression, with Electrographic seizures frequent on EEG (~10/h).

Current treatment and adherence: Lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily; brivaracetam 100 mg twice daily introduced 3 months ago with partial daytime sedation. Adherence is generally good but a couple of missed evening doses coincided with back-to-back night shifts. Caffeine use has increased. Alcohol minimal. No recreational drugs.

Risk and safety: Deterioration is evident clinically and electrophysiologically. We discussed SUDEP and general safety planning, including supervised bathing, avoiding heights, and breaks at work when prodromal symptoms occur. Patient reports no current driving; DVLA advice previously followed.

Plan:
- Escalate contact and monitoring: arrange epilepsy nurse telephone review within 2 weeks and then monthly for the next quarter to track seizure clusters and tolerability.
- Sleep and shift-work mitigation: patient-reported link between night shifts/curtailed sleep and worsening; provided written advice on sleep consolidation strategies and asked employer for an occupational health review to consider temporary rotation away from consecutive nights if feasible.
- Medication optimisation: discuss next-line options at MDT, including introduction of a third agent with a view to rationalisation if response seen; patient open to trial of perampanel or zonisamide after MDT advice. Baseline weight and mood screening to be repeated prior to any change.
- Further diagnostics: repeat ambulatory EEG in 6–8 weeks if deterioration persists despite sleep adjustments, to reassess seizure density. Cardiorespiratory screen not indicated presently; will re-evaluate if events evolve.
- Safety: reinforced seizure first aid; advised keeping a detailed seizure and sleep diary (time to bed, total sleep time, shift pattern, caffeine), to be reviewed at next contact.

I am concerned about the recent deterioration and the objective finding of frequent electrographic seizures. I have requested an earlier follow-up in 6–8 weeks rather than routine, with interim nurse contact as above. Please let me know if problems arise sooner.

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter clearly documents ongoing seizures with a quantified rate during prolonged video-EEG: '~10/h'. There is also qualitative reporting of increased brief events, sometimes clustering, but no precise daily, weekly, or monthly counts. Allowed output units do not include 'hour', and there is insufficient information to reliably extrapolate an hourly rate to a daily rate without speculation. Following the instruction to avoid speculation when exact numbers cannot be determined in the required units, the safest accurate normalization is 'multiple per day'.
